text: These cadaveric dissections demonstrate the anatomy required to understand mediastinoscopy.
The pre-tracheal fascia has to be opened to enter the pre-tracheal plane.
The mediastinoscope enters subjacent to the pre-tracheal fascia.
Note that the lymph nodes are outside the pre-tracheal fascia.
At the carina, note the proximity of the white pulmonary artery and the azagus vein.
A close-up view of this area demonstrates the anatomy of the pulmonary artery just anterior to the subcarinal space.
and the anatomy of the recurrent nerve near the left tracheobronchial lymph nodes.
The narrow cup forceps elongated gives a better view than the larger cup forceps seen on the top.
Patient's neck is hyperextended and he is placed in the somewhat reversed Trendelenburg position.
Standard neck preparation is utilized.
A short transverse incision one centimeter above the suprasternal notch is used.
Only an incision the size of the scope is required.
Dissection only in the vertical plane in the midline.
avoids all vessels.
And separating the strap muscles in the midline continues the dissection.
The median raphe between strap muscles is opened until the trachea is reached and the pre-tracheal fascia is opened.
The plane underneath the pretracheal fascia is further open first by scissors and then finger dissection down to the carina.
This finger dissection breaks through the pretracheal fascia to release lymph nodes external to this fascia.
The mediastinoscope can now be inserted most easily with the opening inferiorly.
All dissection is carried out with the open-tipped blunt sucker.
Maintaining the mediastinoscope just in front of the trachea allows inspection of the whole proximal trachea and carina.
noting the lymph nodes on either side of the pre-tracheal space until the carina is reached anterior to which is the pulsating pulmonary artery.
These lymph nodes are now dissected individually and biopsied.
A minimum of two ipsilateral node stations, one contralateral node station, and a subcarinal node station is biopsied.
The left main stem bronchus with a lymph node and the recurrent nerve seen to the left.
The left paratracheal gutter.
The subcarinal space with lymph nodes seen posteriorly and pulmonary artery anteriorly, the right main stem bronchus.
Lymph nodes are now dissected with a sucker and individually biopsied, labeled, and sent for pathologic identification.
An obviously enlarged, involved lymph node containing metastatic carcinoma.
a series of enlarged lymph nodes all containing metastatic carcinoma.
Care must be taken near the azagus vein seen to the right of an enlarged lymph node before biopsying that node.
In the subcarinal space, bleeding may ensue after dissection of the lymph nodes.
And along the right main stem bronchus, care must be taken to avoid the pulmonary artery.
Subcarinal lymphadenopathy.
Following biopsy, some bleeding ensues in the subcarinal space.
This can be controlled by application of clips, cauterization, or small pieces of Surgicel.
Note the proximity of the recurrent nerve to left-sided lymph nodes.
Lymph nodes can be dissected and biopsied.
Once finger dissection reveals the plane, the scope is inserted and slowly advanced into the anterior mediastinum.
The aortic pulsations are seen on the right.
And an enlarged lymph node encountered at level six.
The innominate vein is seen superiorly, the innominate artery inferiorly, and the scope is passed between them.
The scope is advanced to the subaortic region where an enlarged lymph node is encountered.
The pleura is opened.
and entered after the lung is deflated using differential ventilation.
The whole pleural space can be inspected and, if necessary, biopsied using a channel thoracoscope.
With this instrument, the whole pleural space, including the diaphragm, can be inspected.
Pleural fluid can be analyzed if this is required in the course of a mediastinoscopy.
On occasion, we have biopsied apical tumors or peripheral tumors utilizing this technique.
The lung is inflated before the scope is withdrawn.
Chest tubes are not required unless the lung has been entered.
The mediastinoscopy incision is closed simply in two layers, the first of platysma and the second of subcutaneous tissue.
A subcuticular skin layer completes the closure.
We do not attempt to approximate the strap muscles.
